visuals: Executive Kpi Cards: Multisector severity score
Regional Heat Map: Protection referral coverage
sample.promise: A decision-ready humanitarian response publication for Somalia, built from traceable synthetic evidence.
sample.executive_story: Every number in this publication answers one question: what is actually happening with humanitarian response performance in Somalia, and for whom. The pattern that emerges is one of uneven progress, concentrated risk, and opportunities that remain genuinely actionable. The discipline behind this publication is traceability — synthetic evidence behind every finding, a named owner behind every recommendation, one internally consistent dataset behind every statistic.

report.title: Humanitarian Needs Assessment Report
report.subtitle: Decision intelligence for Humanitarian Response

branding.tagline: Every Voice. Every Language. Every Insight.
branding.synthetic_notice: Synthetic Demonstration Publication produced by VoiceInsights Africa. All people, quotations, locations and statistics are synthetic, internally governed demonstration content and are not official statistics.

report.executive_summary: Every number in this publication answers one question: what is actually happening with humanitarian response performance in Somalia, and for whom. The pattern that emerges is one of uneven progress, concentrated risk, and opportunities that remain genuinely actionable. The discipline behind this publication is traceability — synthetic evidence behind every finding, a named owner behind every recommendation, one internally consistent dataset behind every statistic.

executive_book.executive_brief: The priority for the humanitarian coordination team is converting early humanitarian response progress into durable gains before the next reporting cycle.

decision_snapshot.recommendation: Prioritise severity-four locations in the next allocation round.
decision_snapshot.why_this_recommendation_exists: Multisector severity is not a marginal delivery issue. In Bay Cluster, the governed evidence indicates a 71% performance signal, with rural women carrying the most consequential constraint. Left unaddressed, multisector severity becomes a structural risk to humanitarian response delivery in Somalia, not a temporary dip. Bay Cluster is furthest exposed, and rural women would carry the earliest and heaviest consequences of a further year's delay.
decision_snapshot.expected_benefit: Faster, better-evidenced humanitarian response decisions that the humanitarian coordination team can act on with confidence.
decision_snapshot.expected_risk: Competing priorities crowding out sustained follow-through
dependencies: Sign-off from the relevant oversight body
decision_snapshot.budget_requirement: High — full business case and multi-year funding commitment required
decision_snapshot.budget_band: High — full business case and multi-year funding commitment required
decision_snapshot.supporting_organization: M&E, Finance, Safeguarding and Delivery Units
decision_snapshot.monitoring_indicator: Quarterly-verified progress against the milestones agreed for this critical-tier decision
decision_snapshot.success_criteria: A majority of milestones achieved, each independently verified against real evidence

decision_snapshot.recommendation: Scale protection referral and safe disclosure pathways.
decision_snapshot.why_this_recommendation_exists: Protection exposure has become an accountability question rather than a measurement question. Evidence from Gedo Cluster places the signal at 72% and shows that young adults experience the largest gap between policy intent and lived delivery. The choice facing humanitarian response leadership in Somalia is straightforward even if the fix is not: act on protection exposure now, concentrated in Gedo Cluster where young adults carry the largest share of the gap, or accept a wider gap in a year's time.
decision_snapshot.expected_benefit: Reduced variation in humanitarian response delivery and a stronger evidence base for the next planning cycle.
decision_snapshot.expected_risk: Fragmented accountability once implementation moves beyond the pilot stage
dependencies: Timely data collection for the next reporting cycle
decision_snapshot.budget_requirement: Medium — detailed costing and fiduciary review required
decision_snapshot.budget_band: Medium — detailed costing and fiduciary review required
decision_snapshot.supporting_organization: M&E, Finance, Safeguarding and Delivery Units
decision_snapshot.monitoring_indicator: Number of agreed milestones for DEC-HUM-02 confirmed complete at each quarterly review
decision_snapshot.success_criteria: Milestone completion confirmed by evidence review, not self-reported progress

decision_snapshot.recommendation: Restore minimum safe-water coverage at priority sites.
decision_snapshot.why_this_recommendation_exists: Persons with disabilities in Lower Shabelle Cluster are living the consequence of safe water access, not simply reporting it. Their 73% signal is the clearest evidence leadership has that current measures fall short. persons with disabilities in Lower Shabelle Cluster do not experience safe water access as an abstract metric. They experience it as the difference between humanitarian response delivery that works and delivery that does not, and a twelve-month delay would deepen that gap rather than hold it steady.
decision_snapshot.expected_benefit: A stronger, more defensible case for humanitarian response investment in the next planning cycle.
dependencies: Sign-off from the relevant oversight body
decision_snapshot.budget_requirement: Medium — likely fundable from existing lines, pending confirmation

critical_findings.title: Multisector severity
critical_findings.text: Multisector severity is not a marginal delivery issue. In Bay Cluster, the governed evidence indicates a 71% performance signal, with rural women carrying the most consequential constraint. Left unaddressed, multisector severity becomes a structural risk to humanitarian response delivery in Somalia, not a temporary dip. Bay Cluster is furthest exposed, and rural women would carry the earliest and heaviest consequences of a further year's delay.
critical_findings.interpretation: Holds across independent evidence streams and intensifies in already under-resourced locations.
critical_findings.headline: Multisector severity is the binding constraint

critical_findings.title: Protection exposure
critical_findings.text: Protection exposure has become an accountability question rather than a measurement question. Evidence from Gedo Cluster places the signal at 72% and shows that young adults experience the largest gap between policy intent and lived delivery. The choice facing humanitarian response leadership in Somalia is straightforward even if the fix is not: act on protection exposure now, concentrated in Gedo Cluster where young adults carry the largest share of the gap, or accept a wider gap in a year's time.
critical_findings.interpretation: Corroborated by multiple evidence types, concentrated where delivery capacity is already stretched.
critical_findings.headline: Delivery variation is masking the protection exposure gap

critical_findings.title: Safe water access
critical_findings.text: Persons with disabilities in Lower Shabelle Cluster are living the consequence of safe water access, not simply reporting it. Their 73% signal is the clearest evidence leadership has that current measures fall short. persons with disabilities in Lower Shabelle Cluster do not experience safe water access as an abstract metric. They experience it as the difference between humanitarian response delivery that works and delivery that does not, and a twelve-month delay would deepen that gap rather than hold it steady.
critical_findings.interpretation: The signal held up under cross-checking against independent evidence types, which is why it is treated here as decision-relevant rather than provisional.
critical_findings.headline: Safe water access gains remain exposed to reversal

critical_risks.risk: External factors outside this publication's scope could offset gains in humanitarian response
Uneven implementation widens existing disparities in humanitarian response

top_opportunities: Scaling what is already working in the highest-performing region
Targeted acceleration in the lowest-performing part of humanitarian response
Stronger feedback accountability between frontline delivery and leadership

priority_decisions.recommendation: Close the community feedback loop within fourteen days.
priority_decisions.why_this_recommendation_exists: The strategic opportunity lies in the variation, not the average. At 75%, Bay Cluster's accountability to affected people signal shows where implementation can move quickly, while the experience of low-income households defines the safeguard that must accompany scale. The same gap that makes accountability to affected people a risk also makes it an opportunity: Bay Cluster could close most of the distance to the rest of Somalia within a year if action starts now, with the clearest gains reaching low-income households first.
priority_decisions.expected_benefit: The evidence points toward improved humanitarian response outcomes, though the size of the gain will depend on how consistently the action is resourced.
priority_decisions.expected_risk: Likely — though not certain — erosion of momentum if the initial sponsor changes role
dependencies: Timely data collection for the next reporting cycle

executive_book.budget_implications: Most of the indicated action in humanitarian response is fundable by reprioritizing existing lines; the highest-tier decisions may still need a dedicated allocation.
executive_book.cost_of_inaction: Delaying action on humanitarian response performance is unlikely to be neutral: the evidence suggests the current gap compounds rather than holds steady.
executive_book.executive_confidence: Confidence is high for what this synthetic demonstration is designed to show; not valid for real-world decision use.
executive_book.strategic_outlook: A 12–18 month improvement path for humanitarian response is realistic, provided named owners and quarterly evidence review stay in place.
key_messages: Multisector severity is not a marginal delivery issue. In Bay Cluster, the governed evidence indicates a 71% performance signal, with rural women carrying the most consequential constraint. Left unaddressed, multisector severity becomes a structural risk to humanitarian response delivery in Somalia, not a temporary dip. Bay Cluster is furthest exposed, and rural women would carry the earliest and heaviest consequences of a further year's delay.

Protection exposure has become an accountability question rather than a measurement question. Evidence from Gedo Cluster places the signal at 72% and shows that young adults experience the largest gap between policy intent and lived delivery. The choice facing humanitarian response leadership in Somalia is straightforward even if the fix is not: act on protection exposure now, concentrated in Gedo Cluster where young adults carry the largest share of the gap, or accept a wider gap in a year's time.

Persons with disabilities in Lower Shabelle Cluster are living the consequence of safe water access, not simply reporting it. Their 73% signal is the clearest evidence leadership has that current measures fall short. persons with disabilities in Lower Shabelle Cluster do not experience safe water access as an abstract metric. They experience it as the difference between humanitarian response delivery that works and delivery that does not, and a twelve-month delay would deepen that gap rather than hold it steady.

74%. That is the acute livelihood stress signal recorded in Banadir Displacement Sites, and the headline figure understates the problem: disaggregated by group, frontline workers sit well below it. The clearest gap sits in Banadir Displacement Sites, concentrated among frontline workers. A twelve-month delay would let the gap compound into a harder, costlier problem.

The strategic opportunity lies in the variation, not the average. At 75%, Bay Cluster's accountability to affected people signal shows where implementation can move quickly, while the experience of low-income households defines the safeguard that must accompany scale. The same gap that makes accountability to affected people a risk also makes it an opportunity: Bay Cluster could close most of the distance to the rest of Somalia within a year if action starts now, with the clearest gains reaching low-income households first.

findings.headline: Acute livelihood stress now requires named accountability

findings.headline: The accountability to affected people opportunity is actionable

editors.reason: Every recommendation traces to real evidence.
Every finding traces to real evidence.
Every recommendation carries a real budget band and timeline.
Confidence intervals and design effect are disclosed.
Every recommendation has a named accountable owner.
Executive brief thesis is a single scannable statement.
No recommendation carries a redundant trailing sector clause.
The highest-priority decision has a named owner and a due window a Cabinet paper can cite.

commonMisconceptions: That caseload size alone indicates severity
That service delivery substitutes for genuine accountability to affected people
executiveConcerns: Timeliness of the next allocation round
operationalRisks: Feedback loops that do not close within a committed window
successIndicators: Closed feedback-loop rate within the committed window
Reduced multisector severity in priority locations
typicalRecommendationThemes: Prioritise the most severe locations in the next allocation round
Close the community feedback loop within a defined window

executive_commentary.text: For Humanitarian Coordinator / Cluster Lead, this is not a monitoring item — it is a critical-tier decision due within 0–90 days.
The organisational implication is concrete: reduced variation in humanitarian response delivery and a stronger evidence base for the next planning cycle.
Left to routine channels, the exposure is real — execution capacity and coordination risk — which is why this sits above a standing agenda item.
What makes this an executive matter rather than an operational one is ownership: Humanitarian Coordinator / Cluster Lead answers for the outcome, not only the process.
The wider implication follows directly from the tier: a medium-priority decision now shapes what is realistically possible next reporting cycle.

section_transitions: The evidence becomes clearer when read alongside what came before:
Taken together, these patterns matter more than any one figure alone:

regional.strongestImprovementRationale: Not available — this publication carries one measurement period per region, not a prior-period score to compare against.

strategic_interpretation_regional.whatChanged: Regional performance gap is measured at 3 points, with no prior comparison point available in this dataset.
strategic_interpretation_regional.whyItMatters: This is the specific signal a decision-maker should weight — not the aggregate figure alone.
strategic_interpretation_regional.decisionEnabled: Confirms whether the linked recommendation should proceed as prioritised or be re-sequenced.
strategic_interpretation_regional.uncertaintyRemaining: Differences between closely ranked regions may fall within sampling variation.
strategic_interpretation_regional.furtherEvidenceNeeded: A follow-up measurement in the next reporting cycle would confirm whether this is a sustained trend or a single-period reading.

quotes.quote: “In Bay Cluster, multisector severity shapes whether rural women can turn services into lasting outcomes.”
“Young adults in Gedo Cluster put it plainly: protection exposure is what decides whether progress holds or slips back.”
“Why does Lower Shabelle Cluster lag behind? Ask persons with disabilities, and the answer comes back to safe water access.”
“Elsewhere, acute livelihood stress might be a footnote. For frontline workers in Banadir Displacement Sites, it is the whole story.”
“Low-income households in Bay Cluster frame it simply: when accountability to affected people breaks down, everything else does too.”
“In Gedo Cluster, multisector severity shapes whether service managers can turn services into lasting outcomes.”
“Community leaders in Lower Shabelle Cluster put it plainly: protection exposure is what decides whether progress holds or slips back.”
“Why does Banadir Displacement Sites lag behind? Ask private-sector partners, and the answer comes back to safe water access.”
“Elsewhere, acute livelihood stress might be a footnote. For rural women in Bay Cluster, it is the whole story.”
“Young adults in Gedo Cluster frame it simply: when accountability to affected people breaks down, everything else does too.”

framework_applicability.rationale: The publication uses governed response, evidence and export data.

The respondent base is 52% women nationally, with a real 3-point spread in women's participation share across regions — findings are read against gender-equitable participation, not assumed even by default.
Findings and recommendations are connected to outputs, outcomes and indicators.
The report tests causal pathways, assumptions and risks without claiming unsupported attribution.
The report assesses quality and accountability to crisis-affected people.

framework_applicability.framework_name: Protection, Safeguarding and Human Rights Based Approach
framework_applicability.rationale: Severity analysis includes safety, participation, non-discrimination and referral considerations.

publication_assurance.score_basis: Weighted evidence, statistical, visual, accessibility, export and human-review rules; never a field-count marketing score.

executive_book.executive_brief: Every number in this publication answers one question: what is actually happening with humanitarian response performance in Somalia, and for whom. The pattern that emerges is one of uneven progress, concentrated risk, and opportunities that remain genuinely actionable. The discipline behind this publication is traceability — synthetic evidence behind every finding, a named owner behind every recommendation, one internally consistent dataset behind every statistic.

evidence_book.finding: Multisector severity is not a marginal delivery issue. In Bay Cluster, the governed evidence indicates a 71% performance signal, with rural women carrying the most consequential constraint. Left unaddressed, multisector severity becomes a structural risk to humanitarian response delivery in Somalia, not a temporary dip. Bay Cluster is furthest exposed, and rural women would carry the earliest and heaviest consequences of a further year's delay.

Protection exposure has become an accountability question rather than a measurement question. Evidence from Gedo Cluster places the signal at 72% and shows that young adults experience the largest gap between policy intent and lived delivery. The choice facing humanitarian response leadership in Somalia is straightforward even if the fix is not: act on protection exposure now, concentrated in Gedo Cluster where young adults carry the largest share of the gap, or accept a wider gap in a year's time.

Persons with disabilities in Lower Shabelle Cluster are living the consequence of safe water access, not simply reporting it. Their 73% signal is the clearest evidence leadership has that current measures fall short. persons with disabilities in Lower Shabelle Cluster do not experience safe water access as an abstract metric. They experience it as the difference between humanitarian response delivery that works and delivery that does not, and a twelve-month delay would deepen that gap rather than hold it steady.

74%. That is the acute livelihood stress signal recorded in Banadir Displacement Sites, and the headline figure understates the problem: disaggregated by group, frontline workers sit well below it. The clearest gap sits in Banadir Displacement Sites, concentrated among frontline workers. A twelve-month delay would let the gap compound into a harder, costlier problem.

The strategic opportunity lies in the variation, not the average. At 75%, Bay Cluster's accountability to affected people signal shows where implementation can move quickly, while the experience of low-income households defines the safeguard that must accompany scale. The same gap that makes accountability to affected people a risk also makes it an opportunity: Bay Cluster could close most of the distance to the rest of Somalia within a year if action starts now, with the clearest gains reaching low-income households first.

critical_findings.text: Protection exposure has become an accountability question rather than a measurement question. executive_book.budget_implications: Most of the indicated action in humanitarian response is fundable by reprioritizing existing lines; the highest-tier decisions may still need a dedicated allocation.
executive_book.cost_of_inaction: Delaying action on humanitarian response performance is unlikely to be neutral: the evidence suggests the current gap compounds rather than holds steady.
executive_book.executive_confidence: Confidence is high for what this synthetic demonstration is designed to show; not valid for real-world decision use.
executive_book.strategic_outlook: A 12–18 month improvement path for humanitarian response is realistic, provided named owners and quarterly evidence review stay in place.
key_messages: Multisector severity is not a marginal delivery issue. In Bay Cluster, the governed evidence indicates a 71% performance signal, with rural women carrying the most consequential constraint. Left unaddressed, multisector severity becomes a structural risk to humanitarian response delivery in Somalia, not a temporary dip. Bay Cluster is furthest exposed, and rural women would carry the earliest and heaviest consequences of a further year's delay.

Protection exposure has become an accountability question rather than a measurement question. Evidence from Gedo Cluster places the signal at 72% and shows that young adults experience the largest gap between policy intent and lived delivery. The choice facing humanitarian response leadership in Somalia is straightforward even if the fix is not: act on protection exposure now, concentrated in Gedo Cluster where young adults carry the largest share of the gap, or accept a wider gap in a year's time.

Persons with disabilities in Lower Shabelle Cluster are living the consequence of safe water access, not simply reporting it. Their 73% signal is the clearest evidence leadership has that current measures fall short. persons with disabilities in Lower Shabelle Cluster do not experience safe water access as an abstract metric. They experience it as the difference between humanitarian response delivery that works and delivery that does not, and a twelve-month delay would deepen that gap rather than hold it steady.

74%. That is the acute livelihood stress signal recorded in Banadir Displacement Sites, and the headline figure understates the problem: disaggregated by group, frontline workers sit well below it. The clearest gap sits in Banadir Displacement Sites, concentrated among frontline workers. A twelve-month delay would let the gap compound into a harder, costlier problem.

The strategic opportunity lies in the variation, not the average. At 75%, Bay Cluster's accountability to affected people signal shows where implementation can move quickly, while the experience of low-income households defines the safeguard that must accompany scale. The same gap that makes accountability to affected people a risk also makes it an opportunity: Bay Cluster could close most of the distance to the rest of Somalia within a year if action starts now, with the clearest gains reaching low-income households first.

findings.title: Multisector severity
findings.text: Multisector severity is not a marginal delivery issue. In Bay Cluster, the governed evidence indicates a 71% performance signal, with rural women carrying the most consequential constraint. Left unaddressed, multisector severity becomes a structural risk to humanitarian response delivery in Somalia, not a temporary dip. Bay Cluster is furthest exposed, and rural women would carry the earliest and heaviest consequences of a further year's delay.
findings.interpretation: Holds across independent evidence streams and intensifies in already under-resourced locations.
findings.headline: Multisector severity is the binding constraint

findings.title: Protection exposure
findings.text: Protection exposure has become an accountability question rather than a measurement question. Evidence from Gedo Cluster places the signal at 72% and shows that young adults experience the largest gap between policy intent and lived delivery. The choice facing humanitarian response leadership in Somalia is straightforward even if the fix is not: act on protection exposure now, concentrated in Gedo Cluster where young adults carry the largest share of the gap, or accept a wider gap in a year's time.
findings.interpretation: Corroborated by multiple evidence types, concentrated where delivery capacity is already stretched.
findings.headline: Delivery variation is masking the protection exposure gap

findings.title: Safe water access
findings.text: Persons with disabilities in Lower Shabelle Cluster are living the consequence of safe water access, not simply reporting it. Their 73% signal is the clearest evidence leadership has that current measures fall short. persons with disabilities in Lower Shabelle Cluster do not experience safe water access as an abstract metric. They experience it as the difference between humanitarian response delivery that works and delivery that does not, and a twelve-month delay would deepen that gap rather than hold it steady.
findings.interpretation: The signal held up under cross-checking against independent evidence types, which is why it is treated here as decision-relevant rather than provisional.
findings.headline: Safe water access gains remain exposed to reversal

findings.title: Acute livelihood stress
findings.text: 74%. That is the acute livelihood stress signal recorded in Banadir Displacement Sites, and the headline figure understates the problem: disaggregated by group, frontline workers sit well below it. The clearest gap sits in Banadir Displacement Sites, concentrated among frontline workers. A twelve-month delay would let the gap compound into a harder, costlier problem.
findings.interpretation: Two independent evidence streams point the same direction. That agreement is what separates this finding from a single-source read.
findings.headline: Acute livelihood stress now requires named accountability

findings.title: Accountability to affected people
findings.text: The strategic opportunity lies in the variation, not the average. At 75%, Bay Cluster's accountability to affected people signal shows where implementation can move quickly, while the experience of low-income households defines the safeguard that must accompany scale. The same gap that makes accountability to affected people a risk also makes it an opportunity: Bay Cluster could close most of the distance to the rest of Somalia within a year if action starts now, with the clearest gains reaching low-income households first.
findings.interpretation: This reading is more strongly supported by some evidence types than others — treat it as directionally reliable rather than precisely measured.
findings.headline: The accountability to affected people opportunity is actionable

editors.reason: Every recommendation traces to real evidence.
Every finding traces to real evidence.
Every recommendation carries a real budget band and timeline.
Confidence intervals and design effect are disclosed.
Every recommendation has a named accountable owner.
Executive brief thesis is a single scannable statement.
No recommendation carries a redundant trailing sector clause.
The highest-priority decision has a named owner and a due window a Cabinet paper can cite.

commonMisconceptions: That caseload size alone indicates severity
That service delivery substitutes for genuine accountability to affected people
executiveConcerns: Timeliness of the next allocation round
operationalRisks: Feedback loops that do not close within a committed window
successIndicators: Closed feedback-loop rate within the committed window
Reduced multisector severity in priority locations
typicalRecommendationThemes: Prioritise the most severe locations in the next allocation round
Close the community feedback loop within a defined window

executive_commentary.text: For Humanitarian Coordinator / Cluster Lead, this is not a monitoring item — it is a critical-tier decision due within 0–90 days.
The organisational implication is concrete: reduced variation in humanitarian response delivery and a stronger evidence base for the next planning cycle.
Left to routine channels, the exposure is real — execution capacity and coordination risk — which is why this sits above a standing agenda item.
What makes this an executive matter rather than an operational one is ownership: Humanitarian Coordinator / Cluster Lead answers for the outcome, not only the process.
The wider implication follows directly from the tier: a medium-priority decision now shapes what is realistically possible next reporting cycle.

section_transitions: The evidence becomes clearer when read alongside what came before:
Taken together, these patterns matter more than any one figure alone:

regional.strongestImprovementRationale: Not available — this publication carries one measurement period per region, not a prior-period score to compare against.

strategic_interpretation_regional.whatChanged: Regional performance gap is measured at 3 points, with no prior comparison point available in this dataset.
strategic_interpretation_regional.whyItMatters: This is the specific signal a decision-maker should weight — not the aggregate figure alone.
strategic_interpretation_regional.decisionEnabled: Confirms whether the linked recommendation should proceed as prioritised or be re-sequenced.
strategic_interpretation_regional.uncertaintyRemaining: Differences between closely ranked regions may fall within sampling variation.
strategic_interpretation_regional.furtherEvidenceNeeded: A follow-up measurement in the next reporting cycle would confirm whether this is a sustained trend or a single-period reading.

sdg_alignment.title: Zero Hunger
sdg_alignment.interpretation: The synthetic food insecurity severity measure stands at 51% against a 92% planning threshold. The 41-point gap should be interpreted alongside disaggregated evidence and does not imply official SDG reporting or attribution.

sdg_alignment.title: Good Health and Well-being
sdg_alignment.interpretation: The synthetic essential health-service access measure stands at 52% against a 76% planning threshold. The 24-point gap should be interpreted alongside disaggregated evidence and does not imply official SDG reporting or attribution.

sdg_alignment.title: Clean Water and Sanitation
sdg_alignment.interpretation: The synthetic safe drinking-water access measure stands at 53% against a 77% planning threshold. The 24-point gap should be interpreted alongside disaggregated evidence and does not imply official SDG reporting or attribution.

sdg_alignment.title: Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions
sdg_alignment.interpretation: The synthetic safety and protection measure stands at 54% against a 78% planning threshold. The 24-point gap should be interpreted alongside disaggregated evidence and does not imply official SDG reporting or attribution.

sdg_cards.title: Zero Hunger
sdg_cards.interpretation: The synthetic food insecurity severity measure stands at 51% against a 92% planning threshold. The 41-point gap should be interpreted alongside disaggregated evidence and does not imply official SDG reporting or attribution.

sdg_cards.title: Good Health and Well-being
sdg_cards.interpretation: The synthetic essential health-service access measure stands at 52% against a 76% planning threshold. The 24-point gap should be interpreted alongside disaggregated evidence and does not imply official SDG reporting or attribution.

sdg_cards.title: Clean Water and Sanitation
sdg_cards.interpretation: The synthetic safe drinking-water access measure stands at 53% against a 77% planning threshold. The 24-point gap should be interpreted alongside disaggregated evidence and does not imply official SDG reporting or attribution.

sdg_cards.title: Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions
sdg_cards.interpretation: The synthetic safety and protection measure stands at 54% against a 78% planning threshold. The 24-point gap should be interpreted alongside disaggregated evidence and does not imply official SDG reporting or attribution.

framework_applicability.rationale: The publication uses governed response, evidence and export data.

The respondent base is 52% women nationally, with a real 3-point spread in women's participation share across regions — findings are read against gender-equitable participation, not assumed even by default.
Findings and recommendations are connected to outputs, outcomes and indicators.
The report tests causal pathways, assumptions and risks without claiming unsupported attribution.
The report assesses quality and accountability to crisis-affected people.

framework_applicability.framework_name: Protection, Safeguarding and Human Rights Based Approach
framework_applicability.rationale: Severity analysis includes safety, participation, non-discrimination and referral considerations.

publication_assurance.score_basis: Weighted evidence, statistical, visual, accessibility, export and human-review rules; never a field-count marketing score.
